description: Figure 5 presents room-temperature engineering and true stress–strain curves for Fe–20Mn–10Al–5Cr–1.5C (mass%) alloys annealed at 1100 °C for 15 min followed by water quenching (WQ, ~1100 °C/s) and air cooling (AC, ~15 °C/s). Panel (a) shows engineering stress versus strain: the AC specimen exhibits a pronounced upper yield point near 1050 MPa followed by nearly constant flow stress around 1020–1040 MPa and fractures at approximately 40 % strain, whereas the WQ specimen displays a lower yield strength (~750 MPa) but extensive work-hardening, reaching ~950 MPa before failure at ~65 % strain. Panel (b) presents the corresponding true stress–true strain curves, where the WQ specimen shows almost linear work-hardening, while the AC specimen exhibits near-zero work-hardening below ~5 % true strain followed by gradual hardening. The marked contrast indicates that slower cooling (AC) promotes nano-sized κ-carbide precipitation, raising yield strength but reducing ductility and work-hardening capacity compared with rapid quenching (WQ), supporting the precipitation-hardening mechanism in these low-density Fe–Mn–Al–C-based alloys.
